Environmental Impact Statement, Proposed Construction of the Downtown Loop Freeway at Greenville, South Carolina

The proposed Downtown Loop Freeway in Greenville, South Carolina, aims to improve travel safety and accessibility by connecting I-385 to U.S. 123 over a 3.5-mile stretch. The project promises to enhance commercial activity in the downtown area but will displace numerous families, businesses, and churches. While the freeway is expected to improve traffic flow and emergency response times, it will introduce noise and air pollution to the corridor. The project has faced opposition due to its impact on historic areas and recreational spaces, but adjustments have been made to minimize harm.

The Downtown Loop Freeway project in Greenville was proposed to enhance transportation efficiency and stimulate economic growth in the downtown area. However, it faced significant public opposition due to concerns over displacement of residents and businesses, as well as potential damage to historic sites and green spaces. Despite these challenges, modifications were made to the plan to address some of the community's concerns, allowing the project to move forward.
"Strong opposition was voiced to any location in that area." — Public Reaction Summary

"The Department staff considered that the great opposition due to the apparent historical significance of the James Street area warranted a shift in the preferred alignment." — Summary of Public Hearing Environmental Comments
